title: VRAM is the primary local AI constraint
content: Local LLM inference and diffusion workloads keep model weights and intermediate data in GPU memory. 12GB suits smaller experiments, 16GB is a strong hobbyist target, and 24GB materially expands the models and batch sizes that fit without CPU offload.

title: CUDA ecosystem preference
content: Many common AI tools are optimized first for NVIDIA CUDA. AMD cards can offer strong raster value, but users who need broad framework compatibility should prioritize CUDA unless they have verified their exact stack.

title: RTX 4090 local AI profile
content: The RTX 4090 combines 24GB VRAM with CUDA support. It is a high-cost option best considered when local AI capacity matters more than power draw or compact size.

title: RTX 4070 Ti Super balanced profile
content: The RTX 4070 Ti Super provides 16GB VRAM and strong 1440p to 4K capability at lower power and cost than flagship GPUs. It is a balanced choice for mixed gaming, creative, and CUDA work.

title: RTX 4070 Super gaming profile
content: The RTX 4070 Super is efficient for high-quality 1440p gaming and supports CUDA, but its 12GB VRAM is less flexible for larger local AI workloads.

title: RX 7900 XTX raster value
content: The RX 7900 XTX offers 24GB VRAM and excellent high-resolution raster performance. It is compelling for 4K gaming when CUDA compatibility is not required.

title: RX 7800 XT 1440p value
content: The RX 7800 XT is a strong value target for 1440p gaming with 16GB VRAM. It favors raster performance per dollar over CUDA ecosystem support.

title: Ryzen 7 9800X3D gaming focus
content: The Ryzen 7 9800X3D is an enthusiast gaming CPU. Its value is strongest in high-refresh gaming systems where the GPU is already well funded.

title: Ryzen 7 7800X3D gaming profile
content: The Ryzen 7 7800X3D remains a focused gaming option with lower platform power than many high-core-count alternatives. It is less compelling for heavily threaded workstation tasks.

title: Ryzen 9 7900X workstation profile
content: Twelve cores and strong productivity performance make the Ryzen 9 7900X suitable for compilation, rendering, containers, and mixed workstation use. It requires stronger cooling than 65W parts.

title: Ryzen 7 7700 balanced profile
content: The Ryzen 7 7700 combines eight cores with a 65W rating. It is a quiet, efficient foundation for development and balanced builds with a useful AM5 upgrade path.

title: Ryzen 5 7600 value profile
content: The Ryzen 5 7600 preserves AM5 upgradeability while leaving more of a constrained gaming budget for the GPU.

title: Core i7-14700K productivity profile
content: The Core i7-14700K offers high multithreaded throughput for editing and compilation, but its peak power and cooling requirements are substantial.

title: Memory capacity for development
content: Docker, local databases, IDEs, browsers, and emulators compete for memory. 32GB is a practical baseline; 64GB provides meaningful headroom for heavier container and mobile build workflows.

title: System RAM for local AI
content: System RAM does not replace VRAM, but 64GB or more helps with model loading, CPU offload, data preparation, and running supporting services beside a local model.

title: 64GB DDR5 workload fit
content: A 64GB DDR5 kit is the default workstation capacity for local AI, development, and video editing when the budget allows.

title: 32GB DDR5 value fit
content: A 32GB DDR5 kit is sufficient for gaming and general productivity, and it protects budget for the GPU in performance-focused gaming builds.

title: Power supply headroom rule
content: A power supply should exceed estimated sustained system draw by at least 35 percent. Additional headroom can reduce fan noise and preserve room for future GPU upgrades.

title: 850W Gold mainstream high-end fit
content: An 850W Gold PSU is a common fit for high-end single-GPU systems when it satisfies the 1.35x power rule. It offers more upgrade margin than 750W without flagship PSU cost.

title: 1000W Gold enthusiast fit
content: A 1000W Gold PSU is appropriate for higher-draw GPUs and workstation CPUs where 850W would leave limited transient or upgrade headroom.

title: Case clearance constraints
content: GPU length, air-cooler height, motherboard form factor, and PSU form factor are hard physical constraints. These values must be checked deterministically before a case is accepted.

title: Fractal North balanced airflow
content: The Fractal North supports full-size ATX components with 355mm GPU clearance and 170mm air-cooler clearance. It balances airflow, acoustics, and upgrade room.

title: NR200P small-form-factor constraints
content: The NR200P is a Mini-ITX enclosure that requires an SFX power supply and limits tower cooler height. Compact builds require stricter part filtering and usually carry a price premium.

title: Cooling capacity rule
content: A cooler's thermal rating must meet or exceed the CPU power rating. Quiet builds benefit from additional capacity because the cooler can dissipate heat at lower fan speed.

title: Peerless Assassin value cooling
content: The Peerless Assassin 120 SE offers high air-cooling capacity at low cost. It is well suited to mainstream and many high-tier CPUs when case height allows it.

title: 360mm AIO high-power cooling
content: A 360mm AIO provides additional thermal capacity for high-power workstation CPUs, but costs more and requires a compatible radiator mount.

title: 1080p gaming allocation
content: At 1080p and high refresh rates, CPU frame delivery matters more than at higher resolutions. GPU budget remains important, but extreme GPU spending can produce diminishing returns.

title: 1440p gaming allocation
content: 1440p gaming usually benefits from a GPU-first budget with a balanced modern CPU. It is the broadest value target for high-refresh PC gaming.

title: 4K gaming allocation
content: At 4K the GPU is normally the primary performance constraint. Budget should favor the graphics card while retaining enough CPU, cooling, and PSU quality to avoid platform imbalance.

title: NVMe capacity for workstation use
content: Development and editing systems benefit from 2TB or more of fast NVMe storage for repositories, containers, caches, footage, and active projects.

title: AM5 platform upgradeability
content: AM5 boards provide a clearer CPU upgrade path than mature end-of-cycle platforms. The value is highest when the user explicitly plans a future CPU upgrade.

title: B650 platform fit
content: A well-equipped B650 motherboard is a sensible mainstream AM5 choice with DDR5 and multiple NVMe slots. Enthusiast CPUs may justify stronger power delivery or an X670E board.

title: X670E workstation expansion
content: X670E boards add expansion and connectivity for high-tier AM5 workstations. They should be selected for actual I/O or upgrade needs rather than prestige alone.

title: Core i7-14700K high-refresh gaming profile
content: The Core i7-14700K is an Intel LGA1700 option for high-refresh gaming and mixed productivity. Its high peak power requires substantial cooling, and the mature socket has less future CPU upgrade room than newer platforms.

title: White Z790 platform pairing
content: A Z790 DDR5 motherboard is the appropriate enthusiast chipset pairing for an unlocked Intel LGA1700 CPU. White or silver heatsink variants can satisfy a light-color build without changing socket or memory compatibility.

title: Planning a white RGB build
content: A coherent white build should prioritize visible components such as the case, graphics card, motherboard heatsinks, memory, and cooler. RGB is an appearance preference and must never override socket, clearance, cooling, or PSU safety rules.

title: 4K 240Hz target trade-off
content: A 4K 240Hz target is exceptionally demanding and depends heavily on the game, settings, and upscaling. The recommendation should maximize GPU headroom without claiming that every title will sustain 240 FPS.
